title: Arroz con pollo
subtitle: Caribbean one-pot chicken and saffron rice
excerpt: Chicken pieces browned and cooked with rice in a sofrito broth of tomato, pepper, and cumin with a little saffron. The great Latin American one-pot dish in its Caribbean expression.
sourceNotes: Arroz con pollo is made across Latin America and the Caribbean, with each country having a distinct version. The Cuban and Puerto Rican versions use the sofrito base of onion, green pepper, garlic, cumin, and oregano, with tinned tomatoes and chicken stock for the rice liquid. Saffron gives the dish its golden colour in the Cuban version; some recipes use turmeric as a cheaper substitute. The dish should produce rice with distinct grains, not risotto-style creaminess.

content.text: Arroz con pollo turns up in some form in almost every Latin American country. The Caribbean version sits on a sofrito of onion, green pepper, garlic, cumin, and oregano. Rice cooks in the same pot as the chicken and takes on the juices. Saffron is the Cuban touch but optional. Turmeric gives a close colour for less money.

Brown the chicken properly before adding the sofrito and rice, the Maillard crust on the skin contributes flavour that makes a real difference in the finished dish. The rice needs exactly the right amount of liquid: enough to cook through, not so much that the grains become waterlogged. Measure carefully and don't lift the lid during the final steaming phase.

Season the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs and {{chicken-drumsticks}} drumsticks with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Brown the chicken skin-side down for 5 minutes until golden. Turn and brown the other side for 3 minutes. Remove and set aside.

In the same pot, add the {{onion}} onion, {{pepper-red}} red pepper, and {{pepper-green}} green pepper. Cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, and {{oregano-dried}} of oregano. Stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and cook for 3 minutes, stirring to break them up.

Add the {{long-grain-rice}} of rinsed rice, {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, and saffron water if using. Stir to combine. Nestle the browned chicken pieces back into the pot, skin-side up, pushing them into the rice. Bring to a boil, then reduce to the lowest simmer. Cover tightly and cook for 20 to 22 minutes until the rice has absorbed the liquid and the chicken is cooked through. Rest covered for 5 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Do not lift the lid during the 20-minute steam. If rice is undercooked after resting, return to the lowest heat covered for 5 more minutes.
items.cause: Too much stock; lid opened during cooking

items.fix: The lowest simmer is required after the initial boil. A slightly caught bottom is not unusual and can have a pleasant toasted flavour, but a black layer is heat too high.
items.cause: Heat too high during rice cooking

items.fix: Nestle the chicken pieces skin-side up on the surface of the rice, not buried. The skin above the liquid remains drier and retains its colour.
items.cause: Chicken submerged rather than sitting on top of rice
items.symptom: Chicken skin soft and pale rather than golden

items.fix: Cook the sofrito for a full 5 minutes before adding the rice. The tomato and pepper should be fully softened and slightly reduced.
items.cause: Sofrito not cooked long enough; paprika not bloomed

content.text: Arroz con pollo turns up in every country from Mexico to Argentina. The Caribbean style, with its heavy green-pepper sofrito and saffron, is Cuban and Puerto Rican. In Cuba it is fiesta food and a family Sunday lunch. In Puerto Rico cooks use achiote oil for colour, not saffron. The dish sits where Spanish cooking met Caribbean pantries. Spanish technique, island ingredients.
